text: A three-centimeter transverse incision is made one centimeter below the xiphoid process. The surgeon uses a finger to blindly dissect the back of the sternum, followed by the insertion of a single-port surgery port through the subxiphoid incision. CO₂ insufflation at eight millimeters Hg displaces the lungs and pericardium, creating space behind the sternum. The vessel sealing device is used to detach the thymus from the sternum. The mediastinum is dissected to open the chest cavity bilaterally, and the thymus, thymic tumor, and surrounding fatty tissue anterior to the phrenic nerves are dissected from the pericardium.
The surgeon isolates the brachiocephalic vein and separates the right upper pole of the thymus. The thymic vein is cut using a vessel sealing device.
The surgeon places the resected thymus into a bag within the mediastinum and removes it through the sub-xiphoid incision. A small French drain is inserted through the same incision.
